restaurants.name: Arraval
restaurants.signature_dish: samosas de cordero and macarrones con butifarra
restaurants.quote: Combines traditional Catalan cooking with international influences, offering dishes ranging from escudella to samosas and fried pizza — a culinary crossroads in the heart of the Raval.

restaurants.name: Bar Super
restaurants.quote: Bar Brutal's sister restaurant serving up wine bar small plates in a retro-like setting near Santa Caterina market — veal sweetbreads in chicken jus and a superb natural wine list.

restaurants.name: Berbena
restaurants.quote: One of those places where everything comes together to perfection. The menu changes often, the ingredients are seasonal and very local — a Slow Food Cargols award winner.

restaurants.name: Bornès
restaurants.quote: Updates iconic Catalan dishes from north to south — from the clotxa of Terres de l'Ebre to the trinxat of Cerdanya. Fricandó with seasonal mushrooms and squab cooked 14 hours low-temperature are standouts.

restaurants.name: Contracorrent Bar and Bistro
restaurants.quote: Run with a lot of passion and care by an Italian duo. Expect a one-of-a-kind experience where passion for food meets expertly chosen wines — a Slow Food Cargols award winner with a winning lunch menu.

restaurants.name: Direkte
restaurants.quote: Arnau Muñío's direct and irreverent menu with ambition and inventiveness at Carrer París — a gastro counter uniting the Japanese concept of chef working in front of the diner. Slow Food 2026 guide.

restaurants.name: El Rectangle
restaurants.quote: Has quickly become a favourite among chefs and locals. The food is young and exciting, with an open kitchen counter — young talented chefs who succeed in realising their projects.

restaurants.name: Fishølogy
restaurants.quote: Stands out as a special spot, paying homage to age-old fish cooking methods with a nose-to-tail philosophy applied to seafood — Michelin-starred and Slow Food Cargols award winner.

restaurants.name: Franca
restaurants.quote: Takes Catalan dishes and reworks them in thrilling, modern ways — reinventing tradition without losing respect for it. Three young chefs from high-end restaurants. Slow Food 2026 guide.

restaurants.name: Pompa
restaurants.quote: A small wine-centric restaurant with an elaborate menu of seasonal dishes — hake pil-pil made with cheeks instead of tails from a two-burner stove. Slow Food 2026 guide.

restaurants.name: Seis Cuarenta
restaurants.quote: Eugeni de Diego's tavern at Diagonal 640 — Gillardeau oysters, seafood salpicón, huevos rellenos, and the signature cubanito sandwich. The casual arm of Colmado Wilmot, open 8am to midnight.

restaurants.name: Suru Bar
restaurants.quote: Young, dedicated team and a menu that changes often — unusual ingredients and combinations that surprise. Slow Food 2026 guide new addition.

restaurants.name: Taberna Nardi
restaurants.signature_dish: char-grilled red tuna neck and zamburiñas
restaurants.quote: The irreverent little sister of Bar Pimentel — fresh market product served daily in shared preparations including red tuna carpaccio on soft brioche and zamburiñas with candied cherries.

restaurants.name: Atipical
restaurants.quote: Chef Matteo Bertozzi's creative cooking with technique and sensitivity, and one of the season's best-conceived lunch menus at very adjusted prices in Poblenou.

restaurants.name: Casa Fiero
restaurants.quote: A 1970s Catalan diner throwback that takes the city's Catalan grandma food trend and switches it up — gratinated macaroni stuffed with three cuts of meat. One of 2025's best openings.

restaurants.name: Finorri
restaurants.signature_dish: rice with seafood and crispy socarrat
restaurants.quote: A great date-night destination in the Gothic Quarter — rice bursting with fresh seafood and crispy socarrat alongside Cantabrian anchovies. Bridges territory and popular dishes with elegance.

restaurants.name: Lora
restaurants.quote: Special occasion-coded Middle Eastern eatery at SLS Barcelona — fattoush salad that crunches and bursts with freshness, plus a pomegranate-glazed lamb shoulder. Notable brunch program.

restaurants.name: Glug
restaurants.signature_dish: onion soup with Comté buttons and macaroni croquette
restaurants.quote: A genuine place where you can see the personalities of its owners Beatrice Casella and Ivan Garcia through their cooking and wine selections — onion soup with Comté buttons is legendary. Slow Food Cargols award.

restaurants.name: La Sosenga
restaurants.signature_dish: beans with black trumpet mushrooms and Iberian pork jowl
restaurants.quote: A Catalan kitchen rooted in territory with certain medieval airs — monthly seven-course tasting menu for €35. Slow Food Cargols award winner.

restaurants.name: Margarit
restaurants.quote: The cuisine is honest, made by people who know their stuff — quite elaborate without being sophisticated. Greek roots adapted with personal layers, imaginative twists, and a commendable wine selection.

restaurants.name: Rasoterra
restaurants.quote: Emblematic restaurant in green revolution with its own kitchen garden and zero-km ingredients in the Gothic Quarter. Slow Food Cargols award winner.

restaurants.name: Xavier Pellicer
restaurants.quote: Redefinitions of classic Catalan dishes lightened with a touch of genius — one Michelin star, committed to organic and local produce. Slow Food Cargols award winner.

restaurants.name: Espai Puntal
restaurants.quote: A fair business without excessive profit ambitions — seasonal small plates at Plaça de Sant Cugat with 100% produce from small producers. Slow Food Cargols award winner.

restaurants.name: Âme
restaurants.quote: Mixes French technique with Mediterranean flavors in a way that feels welcoming and never formal — a tasting menu that feels like a special night without being intimidating.

restaurants.name: Besta
restaurants.quote: Focuses heavily on seafood, but not in a traditional way. The dishes are creative without being complicated — reliable, consistently good, with a strong wine list.

restaurants.name: Maleducat
restaurants.signature_dish: arroz seco with pig trotter carpaccio and shrimp tartare
restaurants.quote: Vibrant hotspot led by a young team. Their creative dishes, rooted in local traditions and seasonal ingredients, capture the energy of Barcelona right now.

restaurants.name: Ona
restaurants.quote: Small and intimate, with only six seats. The menu changes constantly depending on what is available that week — one of the most memorable tasting menus in the city.

restaurants.name: Suto
restaurants.quote: Has a Michelin star. Focus is on seasonal seafood and an interesting omakase that feels personal and warm.

restaurants.name: Fonda Pepa
restaurants.quote: Unique gastro bar. Their menu is a fusion of Catalan classics, sprinkled with Mexican-inspired surprises that always delight.

restaurants.name: Can Fisher
restaurants.quote: Offers a delightful seafood experience with stunning sea views. Fresh seafood, grilled delights, and a variety of rice dishes in Poblenou.

restaurants.name: Mercado Central Taberna
restaurants.quote: Barcelona's first dedicated ceviche bar. The kitchen revives rare Peruvian ceviches like the carretillero (street version with chicharrón) and the apaltado, rarely found outside Lima.

restaurants.name: Mineral
restaurants.quote: Cuina radicalment personal — a small space with direct service and a radically personal menu from Oliver Peña and Cristina Losada. Very new opening (late February 2026).

restaurants.name: Fukamura
restaurants.quote: Precision omakase with a ceremonial approach — six-seat counter by Chef Daisuke Fukamura emphasising seasonal ingredients and refined Japanese technique.

restaurants.name: Ultramarinos Marín
restaurants.quote: Hybrid bar, workshop, and charcuterie temple — celebrated as a standout of Barcelona's 2025 restaurant scene connecting the curated charcuterie trend.

restaurants.name: La Palma de Bellafila
restaurants.quote: One of the best meals I have had this year, courtesy of chef Jordi Parramon. The space is very pretty and allows you to have a romantic date — shareable Mediterranean cuisine using local products.

restaurants.name: Mezz
restaurants.quote: What chef Cristina Ojeda Martínez has created is refreshing, modern, and tailored to our palate. A place to keep coming back to for its French-inspired modern cuisine and natural wines.

restaurants.name: Pepi House
restaurants.quote: Traditional tapas, made their way and at a good price. Here they bring all their knowledge to serve a much less gentrified neighborhood in the Clot area.

restaurants.name: Malparit
restaurants.quote: Grilled meats and lots of quality animal protein. They make a capipota to dip bread in, and they are just right with the right amount of spice.

restaurants.name: Granja Elena
restaurants.quote: Family-run for nearly 50 years by chef Borja Sierra near Passeig de la Zona Franca, offering neighbourhood haute cuisine for under €30 per person.

restaurants.name: Bar Iberia
restaurants.quote: Traditional bar run by brothers Francisco and Longinos Álvarez in Sants-Montjuïc serving classic Catalan cuisine in a traditional neighbourhood bar atmosphere.

restaurants.name: El Xampanyet
restaurants.quote: The room is always buzzing, the cava is made in-house, and the food consistently delivers — a classic El Born tapas bar since 1929 with no reservations.

restaurants.name: Doppietta
restaurants.quote: Everything tasted genuinely authentic. It is the kind of place you leave already planning your next visit — Italian pasta, starters, and Aperol spritzes done right.

restaurants.name: Pho Hanoi
restaurants.quote: This is the most authentic Vietnamese food I have found in Barcelona so far. Simple, comforting, dependable — the Bún Huế is exceptional.

restaurants.name: Ali Ocakbasi
restaurants.quote: When I am craving real Turkish flavors, this is where I go. Ideal for ordering multiple dishes and sharing — grilled meats, dips, tea, and dessert.

restaurants.name: Kim BBQ
restaurants.quote: When I am in the mood for Korean food and do not want to think too hard, this is where I land — solid Korean barbecue in Eixample.

restaurants.name: Seoul Nadri
restaurants.quote: Definitely one of the more authentic KBBQ experiences in Barcelona. Reminded me of KTown in Los Angeles.

restaurants.name: Akira Hand Roll Bar
restaurants.quote: Simply the best and most authentic sushi and handrolls in Barcelona. A bit fancy, but worth the money — arrive 15 minutes before opening.

restaurants.name: Yoi Yoi Gion
restaurants.quote: When I want to treat myself a little, this is where I go. Worth it for the handmade udon noodles — upscale Japanese in Eixample.

restaurants.name: Tamarindo
restaurants.quote: Never lets me down. Generous cocktails, tacos and nachos always win, and the evening naturally turns into something fun and long.

restaurants.name: Mundial Bar
restaurants.quote: The group that co-owns Paradiso has turned this old literary haunt into a full-on seafood bar where dinner for two costs €50 and wine flows at €3 a glass.

restaurants.name: Al Grano Bistro
restaurants.quote: The perfect spot for date night with pasta — where expat Italians celebrate birthdays eating linguine with smoked butter and Mediterranean red tuna.

restaurants.name: Chambacú
restaurants.signature_dish: pescado a la talla cooked in banana leaf
restaurants.quote: Barcelona's hottest new Colombian restaurant offers two tasting menus that spotlight Indigenous recipes fused with Spanish colonial and African flavors — pescado a la talla cooked in banana leaf.
